suggestions.name: StringReplacer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused exclusively on string replacement operations, including case-sensitive, case-insensitive, and character-level replacements. Extracting them into a StringReplacer class creates a focused component with high cohesion around the single responsibility of text substitution.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, focused responsibility: performing string replacement operations. They all take an input string and return a modified version where specified characters or substrings have been replaced with new values. The variations include case-sensitive vs case-insensitive replacement, character vs substring replacement, and optional maximum replacement limits.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string replacement" domain concept. They represent different strategies for the same fundamental operation - finding and substituting text within strings. This is a cohesive subsystem focused on text transformation through replacement operations.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates replacement logic from other string utilities
- Code reusability: Replacement functionality can be used independently across different contexts
- Complexity reduction: Removes replacement concerns from the broader StringUtils class
- Testability: Replacement logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns. The replacement operations form a cohesive unit that can be extracted into a specialized class focused solely on string replacement transformations, improving maintainability and adhering to the Single Responsibility Principle.

suggestions.name: ArrayJoiner
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for converting arrays of primitive types into delimited strings. They share common logic patterns, performance optimization strategies, and operate on the same domain concept of array-to-string transformation. Extracting them creates a focused component that encapsulates array joining behavior.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of joining array elements into delimited strings. They all follow the same pattern: take an array of primitive types (byte, char, float, long), a delimiter character, and start/end indices, then produce a single string with elements separated by the delimiter. The capacity() helper method optimizes StringBuilder allocation based on expected output size.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "array-to-string conversion" domain. They represent a specialized form of string formatting that transforms arrays of different primitive types into delimited string representations. The capacity() method provides performance optimization by pre-calculating StringBuilder size based on element count and maximum character width per type.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates array joining logic from other string utilities
- Code reusability: Array joining functionality can be used independently across different contexts
- Complexity reduction: Removes specialized array formatting logic from the general StringUtils class
- Testability: Array joining behavior can be tested in isolation with focused test cases
- Performance optimization: The capacity calculation logic is encapsulated and can be refined independently

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate array formatting concerns. The methods exhibit high cohesion (all perform array-to-string conversion) and can operate with low coupling (minimal dependencies beyond StringBuilder). This creates a focused component following the Single Responsibility Principle.

suggestions.name: StringSplitter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for splitting strings into token arrays using various separator strategies. They share common splitting algorithms, handle similar edge cases, and work together to provide comprehensive string tokenization functionality that deserves its own focused class.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of splitting strings into arrays based on various separator criteria. The core functionality includes: comparing strings for equality (case-sensitive and case-insensitive), splitting strings by whole separators with options to preserve empty tokens, and implementing the underlying splitting logic through worker methods.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string splitting and comparison" domain. They work together to provide comprehensive string splitting capabilities, handling edge cases like null inputs, empty strings, consecutive separators, and maximum token limits. The equality methods support the splitting logic by providing comparison utilities.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string splitting logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: The splitting functionality can be reused across different contexts without importing the entire StringUtils class
- Complexity Reduction: Removes complex splitting algorithms from the main utility class, making both classes more focused
- Testability: Splitting logic can be tested independently with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns. The splitting functionality represents a cohesive unit with well-defined inputs and outputs, making it an ideal candidate for extraction into a specialized class focused solely on string tokenization.

suggestions.name: StringStripper
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on stripping/trimming characters from string boundaries. They share the same core responsibility of removing whitespace or specified characters from the start and end of strings, with various null-safety handling strategies. Extracting them creates a specialized, reusable component that encapsulates string boundary cleaning operations.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: removing whitespace and specified characters from the beginning and end of strings. They collectively accomplish string trimming/stripping operations, handling both single strings and arrays of strings, with various null-safety and empty string handling strategies.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string trimming/stripping" domain. They represent different variations of the same core operation - removing unwanted characters from string boundaries. The methods work together as a cohesive unit, with some methods calling others (strip calls stripStart/stripEnd, stripAll calls strip).

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string stripping logic from other string utilities
- Enhances Reusability: String stripping functionality can be used independently across different contexts
- Reduces Complexity: Removes a focused set of related operations from the large StringUtils class
- Improves Testability: String stripping logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate string trimming concerns. This creates a specialized class with high cohesion (all methods perform string boundary character removal) and low coupling (minimal dependencies, mainly on basic string operations and ArrayUtils).

suggestions.name: TextNormalizer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for normalizing text by removing accents and converting special Unicode characters to their ASCII equivalents. Extracting them into a TextNormalizer class improves separation of concerns by isolating internationalization and character encoding logic from general string manipulation utilities.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
The main responsibility shared by these methods is text normalization and character conversion. The `stripAccents` method removes accented characters from text, `convertRemainingAccentCharacters` handles specific Unicode characters that need manual conversion to their ASCII equivalents, and `replaceAll` performs regex-based text replacement. They collectively accomplish the transformation of text containing special characters into normalized, ASCII-compatible forms.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "text normalization" domain concept. They work together to handle Unicode normalization, accent removal, and character conversion - all aspects of transforming text from various international character sets into standardized ASCII representations. This is a cohesive subsystem focused on character encoding and text standardization.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates text normalization logic from other string utilities
- Code reusability: Text normalization can be reused across different applications needing internationalization support
- Complexity reduction: Removes specialized Unicode handling from the general StringUtils class
- Testability: Allows focused testing of normalization edge cases and Unicode character handling

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns based on the Single Responsibility Principle. The extracted class represents a specialized text transformation capability that operates on a specific domain (Unicode normalization) and can be independently tested and reused.

suggestions.name: WhitespaceNormalizer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on whitespace normalization and trimming operations. They share common patterns for handling null values, empty strings, and various whitespace characters. Extracting them into a WhitespaceNormalizer class creates a specialized component with a single, well-defined responsibility for cleaning and normalizing whitespace in strings.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: cleaning and normalizing whitespace in strings. They all deal with removing, trimming, or normalizing various forms of whitespace characters (spaces, tabs, newlines, etc.) from strings. The methods handle different aspects of whitespace cleanup - normalizing internal spaces, trimming edges, and converting between null/empty representations.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "whitespace normalization" domain. They work together to provide comprehensive whitespace handling capabilities, from basic trimming to advanced normalization that collapses multiple whitespace characters into single spaces. They share the EMPTY constant and have consistent null-safety patterns.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates whitespace handling logic from other string operations
- Enhances Reusability: Whitespace normalization can be used independently across different contexts
- Reduces Complexity: Removes specialized whitespace logic from the general StringUtils class
- Improves Testability: Whitespace normalization logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate whitespace normalization concerns. This creates a specialized class with high cohesion (all methods manipulate whitespace) and low coupling (minimal dependencies beyond basic string operations). The extraction follows the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class focused solely on whitespace normalization.

suggestions.name: StringAbbreviator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for intelligently shortening strings with abbreviation markers. They share complex truncation logic, validation rules, and abbreviation constants, representing a specialized text processing concern that should be separated from general string utilities for better maintainability and reusability.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: shortening strings to fit within specified length constraints while adding abbreviation markers (like ellipsis). All four methods are overloaded versions that provide different ways to abbreviate text - with default markers, custom markers, offsets, or combinations thereof. They collectively accomplish the task of intelligently truncating strings while preserving readability.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods represent the "text abbreviation" domain concept. They form a cohesive subsystem focused on string truncation logic, handling edge cases like minimum width validation, offset positioning, and marker placement. The shared constants (ELLIPSIS3, EMPTY) and the complex logic for determining where to place abbreviation markers demonstrate this is a specialized text processing concern.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates abbreviation logic from the broader StringUtils class
- Code reusability: Abbreviation functionality can be used independently across different contexts
- Complexity reduction: Removes complex abbreviation algorithms from the main utility class
- Testability: Abbreviation logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases
- Maintainability: Changes to abbreviation behavior are localized to one class

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate a cohesive set of related methods that represent a single concern. The Strategy pattern could be applied here as well, as different abbreviation approaches (with/without offset, different markers) represent different strategies for the same goal. This extraction supports the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class with high cohesion around text abbreviation.

suggestions.name: StringPadder
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for string padding and alignment operations. They share common padding logic, constants, and work together to provide left, right, and center padding functionality. Extracting them creates a focused component that encapsulates all string padding concerns.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: formatting strings by adding padding characters to achieve specific widths and alignments. They collectively accomplish string padding operations - adding characters to the left (leftPad), right (rightPad), or both sides (center) of strings to reach desired lengths. The repeat method supports this by generating the padding characters needed.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string formatting" domain, specifically focused on padding and alignment operations. They work together to provide comprehensive string padding capabilities with various padding characters and alignment options. The shared constants (SPACE, EMPTY, PAD_LIMIT) and the repeat utility method all support this cohesive padding functionality.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string padding logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: Padding operations can be reused across different contexts (reports, console output, data formatting)
- Complexity Reduction: Removes specialized formatting logic from the general StringUtils class
- Testability: Padding logic can be tested independently with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate formatting concerns. The methods exhibit high cohesion (all related to string padding/alignment) and can operate independently with minimal external dependencies. This creates a focused component following the Single Responsibility Principle.

suggestions.name: StringTokenizer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive tokenization unit that parses strings into components based on whitespace and custom separators. They share complex parsing logic and work together to provide comprehensive string splitting functionality. Extracting them creates a focused component dedicated to string tokenization and whitespace processing.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a primary responsibility of parsing and manipulating strings based on whitespace and separator characters. The `deleteWhitespace` method removes all whitespace characters, `isWhitespace` checks if a string contains only whitespace, and the various `split` methods break strings into arrays based on whitespace or custom separators. All methods fundamentally deal with identifying, processing, and manipulating whitespace and delimiter characters within strings.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "string tokenization and whitespace processing" domain. They form a cohesive subsystem focused on breaking down strings into meaningful parts by recognizing boundaries (whitespace or custom separators). The `splitWorker` private method serves as the core algorithm that handles the complex logic of tokenization with various options for preserving tokens and handling different separator types.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string parsing/tokenization logic from other string utilities
- Code reusability: Tokenization functionality can be reused across different contexts beyond StringUtils
- Complexity reduction: Removes complex parsing algorithms from the main utility class, making it more focused
- Testability: Tokenization logic can be tested independently with focused test cases for edge cases like empty strings, null separators, and preserve-token scenarios

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate parsing concerns from general string utilities. The extraction creates a specialized component following the Single Responsibility Principle, where the new class has high cohesion (all methods work together to parse strings) and low coupling (minimal dependencies on external string operations).

suggestions.name: StringTransformer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on transforming and manipulating string content through various algorithms and operations. They share the common responsibility of taking string inputs and producing transformed string outputs, involving complex logic like fuzzy matching, multi-pattern replacement, and encoding transformations. Extracting them improves code organization by separating advanced transformation operations from basic string utilities.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of transforming and manipulating string content in various ways. They perform operations like providing default values for null strings, calculating fuzzy string matching scores, joining collections into strings, performing complex multi-pattern replacements, reversing strings, converting to code points, and encoding bytes to strings. All methods take string-like inputs and produce transformed string outputs.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string transformation" domain. They represent different types of string manipulation operations that go beyond basic string operations - they involve algorithmic transformations, formatting, encoding, and advanced text processing. Unlike simple string utilities, these methods implement specific transformation algorithms or complex manipulation logic.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: String transformation logic is isolated from other string utilities
- Enhances Reusability: Transformation operations can be used independently across different contexts
- Reduces Complexity: The original StringUtils class becomes more focused on basic string operations
- Improves Testability: Complex transformation algorithms can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate complex string transformation concerns from basic string utilities. The new class will have high cohesion (all methods transform strings in sophisticated ways) and low coupling (minimal dependencies beyond basic string operations). This aligns with the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a focused class for advanced string transformations.

suggestions.name: StringTrimmer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on removing trailing characters from strings, with specialized handling for line endings. Extracting them into a StringTrimmer class improves separation of concerns by isolating string tail manipulation logic from general string utilities.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: removing characters from the end of strings, specifically handling line ending characters (CR, LF). The `chomp` method removes line terminators from the end of a string, while `chop` removes the last character (with special handling for CRLF sequences). Both methods focus on string truncation operations that deal with trailing characters.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods belong to the "string trimming/truncation" domain, specifically focused on removing trailing content. They share the same constants (EMPTY, LF, CR) and both implement logic to handle different line ending scenarios (CR, LF, CRLF). This represents a cohesive subsystem for string tail manipulation operations.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string tail removal logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: The trimming functionality can be reused across different contexts where line ending handling is needed
- Complexity Reduction: Removes specialized trimming logic from the larger StringUtils class
- Testability: Easier to test edge cases around line ending handling in isolation

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns. The methods represent a cohesive unit of functionality that operates on a specific aspect of strings (trailing characters), making them suitable for extraction into a focused class that handles string tail trimming operations.

suggestions.name: StringExtractor
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on extracting substrings from specific positions (left/right) within strings. Extracting them into a StringExtractor class isolates positional substring operations from general string utilities, improving code organization and creating a reusable component for directional string extraction operations.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: extracting substrings from specific positions within a string. The `left()` method extracts characters from the beginning of a string up to a specified length, while the `right()` method extracts characters from the end of a string up to a specified length. Both handle null inputs and edge cases consistently.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods belong to the "string extraction" or "substring extraction" domain. They represent positional substring operations that extract portions of strings based on directional positioning (left/right) rather than arbitrary start/end indices. They form a cohesive set of operations for extracting string segments from specific positions.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates positional substring extraction logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: These extraction methods can be reused across different contexts that need positional string operations
- Complexity Reduction: Removes specialized substring logic from the larger StringUtils class, making it more focused
- Testability: Positional extraction logic can be tested independently with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns based on functional cohesion. The methods represent a specialized subset of string operations focused on positional extraction, which is a distinct responsibility from general string manipulation utilities.
